name: TitleMax Title Loans
description_short: TitleMax provides short-term vehicle title loans and title pawns, secured by car or motorcycle titles. Loans range $500–$25,000; APRs run 100–300% depending on state.
description_long:
TitleMax was founded September 1, 1998 in Columbus, Georgia and is now headquartered in Savannah, GA, with a secondary office in Carrollton, TX. It operates as a subsidiary of TMX Finance LLC, a privately held company owned by Tracy Young. From a single storefront, TitleMax has grown to approximately 900 locations across 13 states: Alabama, Arizona, Delaware, Georgia, Kansas, Mississippi, Missouri, Nevada, South Carolina, Tennessee, Texas, Utah, and Wisconsin. The company holds no CDFI, HUD, or NFCC certifications, is not BBB-accredited, and has been the subject of two significant Consumer Financial Protection Bureau enforcement actions.

TitleMax's core product is the auto title loan — a short-term loan secured by the borrower's vehicle title. Borrowers retain use of their vehicle while repaying but face repossession upon default. Loan amounts typically range from $500 to $10,000, reaching up to $25,000 in select markets. The company also offers motorcycle title loans, title pawns (a pawn arrangement against vehicle title, available in select states), and personal/signature loans (unsecured, select markets). Interest rates vary by state, ranging from 100% to 300% APR where permitted, with caps only where state law mandates them. Funding is typically disbursed within 30 minutes of approval via debit card or MoneyGram. Approval is based on vehicle value rather than credit score, making TitleMax accessible to borrowers with poor or no credit history.

TitleMax's speed and physical accessibility are its primary differentiators. With nearly 900 storefronts, borrowers can walk in, have their vehicle assessed, and leave with same-day cash — a genuine operational strength for consumers in urgent need. However, its regulatory record is severe. In 2016, the CFPB fined TitleMax $9 million for deceptive practices in Georgia, Tennessee, and Alabama. In 2023, the CFPB levied a $15 million penalty ($10M civil penalty plus $5M in restitution) after finding TitleMax charged active-duty military members nearly triple the legally permitted 36% APR cap under the Military Lending Act, and had doctored borrower records to conceal their military status. Active-duty servicemembers and their dependents are now legally prohibited from using TitleMax.

For credit-poor consumers who own their vehicle outright and have exhausted every other option, TitleMax delivers fast cash without a credit check. Its mobile app, wide store footprint, and 30-minute disbursement are real operational advantages. But triple-digit APRs make these loans brutally expensive for anyone who cannot repay quickly, and the documented history of predatory practices — including falsifying records to evade consumer protections — is a serious red flag. Consumer complaint platforms echo these concerns: ComplaintsBoard rates TitleMax 2.0/5 and PissedConsumer 1.7/5, with recurring issues around repossession conduct and payment application. Borrowers should treat TitleMax strictly as a last resort and calculate the full repayment cost before signing any agreement.

pricing.guarantee_details: No money-back guarantee policy found on website or in verified sources. Not applicable to this loan product type.

pros: Same-day cash disbursement typically within 30 minutes of approval
Approval based on vehicle equity, not credit score — accessible to poor or no credit borrowers
Nearly 900 physical storefronts across 13 states for in-person service
Loan amounts up to $25,000 in select markets — higher ceiling than most title lenders
Multiple collateral loan types available: car title loans, motorcycle title loans, title pawns, and signature loans
Mobile app on iOS and Android for balance checks, payments, and store locator
Google Maps rating of 5.0/5 from 1,127 reviews at the Arlington, TN location
cons: APRs of 100–300% make these among the most expensive legal loans available in the U.S.
Two CFPB enforcement actions: $9M fine in 2016 for deceptive practices, and $15M penalty in 2023 for violating the Military Lending Act and falsifying borrower records
Active-duty military members and their dependents are legally prohibited from borrowing due to 2023 CFPB action
Vehicle repossession risk on default — borrowers can lose their primary transportation
Not BBB-accredited; ComplaintsBoard 2.0/5 and PissedConsumer 1.7/5 with recurring complaints about repossession conduct and payment misapplication
best_for: Consumers with a fully or nearly paid-off vehicle who need emergency cash same-day and cannot qualify for traditional credit
Borrowers with poor or no credit history who have been declined by banks, credit unions, and online personal loan lenders
Short-term cash needs where the borrower has a clear repayment plan within the loan term to avoid compounding triple-digit interest
diagnosis: TitleMax is best suited for credit-poor consumers who own their vehicle outright, need cash immediately, and have no viable lower-cost alternative. Its 30-minute funding and nearly 900-store footprint make it genuinely accessible where other lenders won't go. However, APRs of 100–300%, a documented pattern of predatory practices backed by two federal enforcement actions, and real vehicle repossession risk make this a high-stakes last resort — not a routine borrowing option.
services: Auto title loans secured by car title ($500–$25,000)
Motorcycle title loans secured by motorcycle title
Title pawns (pawn arrangement against vehicle title, select states)
Personal and signature loans (unsecured, select markets)
Same-day cash disbursement within approximately 30 minutes of approval
Bad-credit loan approvals based on vehicle value rather than credit score
Funds delivered via debit card or MoneyGram
In-store vehicle title assessment and valuation
Mobile app account management, payment, and store locator (iOS and Android)
In-person loan consultations at approximately 900 storefront locations
